story:
I came to in a hole seven feet deep, a shovel in my hand, dirt on my boots, and a skeleton at my feet. The night above was all fog and star-pricked branches. Worms worked the soil. One slid out of the skull’s eye. My body was still sturdy for 86, but my mind was a jar of moonshine gone cloudy. War smoke and old screams flashed, then faded. I prayed for the man in the dirt, wondered who he was, how he got here, how I got here—until I noticed my own white hospital gown, a medical band on one wrist, a pair of handcuffs dangling from the other.

A whisper came through the trees. “Pa.” Then, closer, “Pawpaw.” A flashlight bobbed. My grandson Billy leaned over the edge, all scruff and sunken eyes. He didn’t offer a hand. He wanted the gold I’d promised—gold I’d said was “seared” in my memory. I told him he was crazy and to get me back to the home. He told me the hard truth: it wasn’t a home; it was prison. He’d broken us out when I was in the medical ward. He needed that gold, said I’d sworn there was plenty. His voice turned small like when he was a freckled boy. Then he was a man again, pacing, angry. Lights moved in the distance. Dogs began to bark.

I looked down at the bones. The skull wore a rusted chain knotted under the jaw. The sight split my mind wide open. Summer of 1955. A boy named Jesse—my age then, tall and strong—was accused of smiling too long at my mother. My pa and the town men burned hot with hate. They doused Jesse in coal oil, looped a chain around his neck, and raised him up. Some lowered him into the fire and out again. He didn’t scream. He just smiled—a defiant, awful smile—his gold-capped teeth shining against burned skin. I watched. I joked about his dancing feet. I helped dig the hole. We buried him to spare my mother shame. That’s what I told myself. The truth: it was a lynching, a hateful crime. I never let myself see Jesse as human. Even years later, locked in a cell near men like him, I called them animals in my head.

Billy saw the lights and ran. “Sorry,” he whispered, and left me in the pit he’d followed me to. Panic iced my spine. The chain in my hand burned. Pain licked my skin as if the fire had found me. Still, muscle memory stayed true. I swung the chain, caught a branch, and hauled myself up, hand over hand like roping bulls in my wrangling days. I rolled out of the grave, gasping. The barking swelled, real or memory I couldn’t tell. I ran crooked through the trees, thirsty, dizzy. I hit an opening—and my feet failed me. I tumbled back into the same hole.

Something jagged stabbed my lung. Air and blood bubbled at my lips. I thought of legacy—my grandson’s greed, my father’s cruelty, my own cowardice—and the truth I’d buried: the “gold” had always been Jesse’s teeth gleaming in firelight. If they found me, my past would come up with me. If they didn’t, my bones would keep him company under the same dirt I once shoveled over him. I lay bleeding beside what was left of the boy we murdered, feeling the pain I had refused to see, and I waited in the dark, smiling at last without any shine.
